title: Spaghetti alle Vongole
subtitle: Neapolitan clam pasta with white wine, garlic, and parsley
description: Spaghetti alle vongole is one of the most celebrated pasta dishes on the Neapolitan coast. Live clams open in a covered pan with white wine and garlic, releasing a briny cooking liquor that forms the sauce. The pasta finishes directly in this liquor, absorbing every trace of the sea. The whole dish comes together in under twenty minutes. The version here is bianco — in white wine — without tomatoes, which keeps the clam flavour clean and bright.
sourceNotes: Spaghetti alle vongole is a cornerstone of Neapolitan cucina di mare. The bianco (white) version appears in both Marcella Hazan's Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking (1992) and is consistent with the clam pasta tradition documented in Ippolito Cavalcanti's Cucina Teorico-Pratica (1839).

recipeTools.notes: Wide enough to hold all the clams in a single layer with a lid. The clams need space to open without crowding.
For lifting the spaghetti into the sauce.
For purging and rinsing the clams.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking live clams in cold salted water for 20–30 minutes before cooking encourages them to expel any sand or grit from their shells. Essential for a clean-tasting dish.
Starchy, salted cooking water from the pasta pot. Added to the sauce, it thickens and emulsifies the oil and clam liquor into a coating consistency.

ingredients.notes: Palourde or Manila clams — soaked in cold salted water for 20 minutes to purge grit
Half for cooking, half to finish

steps: Purge the clams: place them in a large bowl of cold salted water (1 tbsp salt per litre) for 20–30 minutes. Lift them out — do not tip and pour, or any grit at the bottom of the bowl goes back. Discard any clams with broken shells, or any that remain open after tapping.
Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil. Cook the spaghetti until 3 minutes short of the packet time.
While the pasta cooks, warm half the olive oil in a wide pan over a medium heat. Add the garlic slices and chilli, if using, and cook for 1 minute until fragrant but not browned.

Turn the heat to high. Add the clams and the white wine. Cover immediately and cook for 3–4 minutes, shaking the pan occasionally, until the clams have opened. Remove the lid — discard any clams that have not opened.

Reserve a ladle of pasta water. Transfer the spaghetti to the pan of clams with tongs, 3 minutes before it is fully cooked. Toss over a high heat, adding splashes of pasta water, until the pasta finishes cooking in the clam liquor and the sauce coats each strand — about 2–3 minutes.

Remove from the heat. Drizzle with the remaining olive oil and scatter with parsley. Serve immediately in warm bowls. No Parmesan — the cheese and shellfish combination is strongly discouraged in Italian tradition.

content.text: in cold salted water for 20–30 minutes. This drives out any sand or grit — skipping it risks a gritty final dish. When lifting the clams out of the water, lift them rather than tipping, so any settled grit stays in the bowl.

Set the pasta water on to boil. Warm half of {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over a medium heat. Add the sliced garlic and chilli flakes and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Increase the heat to high. Add {{live-clams}} of clams and {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine. Cover and cook for 3–4 minutes, shaking occasionally, until all the clams are open. Discard any that remain closed.
Finish the pasta in the sauce
Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti until 3 minutes short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of

. Transfer the spaghetti to the clam pan with tongs and toss over a high heat, adding splashes of pasta water, until the pasta finishes cooking and the sauce coats each strand — 2–3 minutes. Remove from the heat, add the remaining olive oil and the parsley, toss once more, and serve immediately.

In Italian cooking, cheese is not served on seafood pasta — it is considered to overwhelm the delicate briny flavour of the shellfish. The combination is one of the more strictly observed conventions in Italian cookery and applies equally to all shellfish pasta dishes.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The two things that most commonly go wrong with vongole.

attrs.solution: The purging step was skipped or too short, or the clams were tipped out of the soaking bowl rather than lifted. Any grit that settled in the bowl goes straight back into the dish when tipped. Next time: lift the clams out individually or with a slotted spoon.

The pasta water was added too generously or the pasta finished cooking before the sauce reduced. Keep the heat high and toss vigorously — the starch in the pasta water and the pasta itself will thicken the liquid quickly.
